topics.meaning: Water is a natural resource needed by all living things. Freshwater is water that is not salty and can be used for drinking, farming and daily needs.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The chapter begins with children watching rain and wondering where water comes from and where it goes. It explains that most of Earth is covered with water, but most of it is salty ocean water. Only a very small part is freshwater, and humans, animals, birds and plants depend on it for survival, crops and daily activities.
topics.easyExplanation: Water is needed for life. We drink it, plants need it to grow, and animals also need it. But we cannot drink sea water directly because it is salty. The useful water for us is freshwater, so we must save it.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Notice water around us
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: We see water as rain, rivers, ponds, lakes, wells and taps.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Understand salty water
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Seas and oceans contain a lot of salt, so their water is not directly drinkable.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Identify freshwater
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Freshwater is found in rivers, lakes, rain, wells, handpumps and underground.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Connect water with life
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: People, animals and plants need freshwater to live and grow.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Use water carefully
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Since freshwater is limited, we should avoid wasting it.

examples: Using water for cooking and cleaning

topics.meaning: Sources of water are the places or forms from where we get water.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The chapter asks students to identify freshwater sources through pictures and observation. These include rain, rivers, lakes, ponds, wells, handpumps, glaciers and groundwater. It also mentions examples like Wular Lake and traditional water storage methods such as johads.
topics.easyExplanation: We get water from many places. Rain brings water. Some water stays in rivers and ponds, some freezes as snow or glaciers, and some goes underground.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Rain falls
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Rain is an important natural source of freshwater.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Water collects
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Rainwater can collect in ponds, lakes and rivers.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Water freezes
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: In cold places, water is stored as snow and glaciers.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Water goes underground
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Some water enters soil and becomes groundwater.

stepByStepExplanation.title: People use sources
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Humans use wells, handpumps, lakes and rivers for water.

topics.meaning: Forms of water are the different states in which water exists: liquid, solid and vapour.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The chapter explains that water is seen as liquid in rain, ponds and rivers; as solid in ice and snow; and as vapour or steam when heated. Activities with ice cubes and boiling water help students observe melting, boiling and droplets forming on a cold glass.
topics.easyExplanation: Water can change its form. Ice melts into water. Water can become steam when heated. Steam can cool and become drops again.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Liquid form
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Water in taps, rain, rivers and ponds is liquid.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Solid form
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: When water freezes, it becomes ice or snow.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Vapour form
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: When water is heated, it becomes water vapour or steam.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Melting
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Ice changes back into liquid water when it gets heat.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Cooling
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Water vapour cools and turns into tiny drops.

topics.meaning: The water cycle is the continuous movement of water from Earth to the sky and back to Earth again.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The chapter uses a transparent bag activity to show how sunlight heats water, water becomes vapour, droplets form inside the bag and then fall back. In nature, the Sun heats water from rivers, lakes, ponds and oceans. Vapour rises, cools, forms clouds and comes down as rain, snow or hail.
topics.easyExplanation: Water keeps moving in a circle. The Sun heats water, water goes up as vapour, clouds form, and water comes back as rain.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Sun heats water
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Sunlight warms water in oceans, rivers, ponds and wet soil.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Evaporation happens
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Some liquid water changes into water vapour and rises.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Cooling happens
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: High in the sky, vapour meets cooler air.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Clouds form
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Tiny droplets join to form clouds.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Water falls
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: When clouds become heavy, rain, snow or hail falls.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Cycle repeats
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Water returns to land and water bodies, then the process starts again.

topics.meaning: Groundwater is water stored under the ground in spaces between soil and rocks. Recharge means filling it again with rainwater.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The chapter explains that when rain falls, some water is soaked by soil and sinks through layers of soil and rocks. This stored underground water is used through wells, borewells, tube wells and handpumps. It also explains that cemented surfaces stop water from entering the ground, while open areas, ponds, soak pits and trees help recharge groundwater.
topics.easyExplanation: Some rainwater goes inside the ground. This water is groundwater. We bring it out using wells and handpumps. To keep groundwater available, rainwater must be allowed to enter the soil.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Rain reaches soil
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Rainwater falls on open ground.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Soil absorbs water
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Water slowly enters pores in soil.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Water moves deeper
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: It passes through soil and rock layers.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Storage happens
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Water gets stored underground as groundwater.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Humans draw water
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Wells, borewells, tube wells and handpumps bring it out.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Recharge is needed
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Open land, trees, ponds and soak pits help water enter the ground again.

importantKeywords.definition: A pit that allows water to seep into the ground.

importantKeywords.definition: A hard paved surface that blocks water from entering soil.

topics.topic: Surface Water and Flow of Rivers
topics.meaning: Surface water is water found on Earth’s surface, such as rivers, ponds, lakes and streams.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The chapter explains that rivers, ponds and lakes are natural freshwater sources. Through an activity with mustard seeds on a folded newspaper, students learn that water flows from higher to lower places and follows the shape of land. Flowing water may stop, curve, collect or change the shape of land.
topics.easyExplanation: Surface water is water we can see on land. Rivers flow down from high places and turn according to the shape of the land.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Water collects on surface
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Rainwater or melted snow may collect in rivers, lakes and ponds.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Higher to lower
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Water naturally flows from high land to low land.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Land guides flow
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Mountains, slopes, rocks and soil decide direction.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Water curves
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Rivers turn when land shape changes.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Land changes
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Flowing water can carry soil and slowly shape land.

examples: A river flowing from a hill
Rainwater running on a sloped road
A pond formed in a low area

topics.meaning: Life in water means plants and animals that live in or near water bodies. Adaptations are special features that help them survive.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The chapter shows ponds and water bodies as habitats for fish, frogs, turtles, water snakes, birds, insects and aquatic plants. It explains that living beings have special features such as fins, long legs, floating leaves, waxy coatings and long roots to live in water.
topics.easyExplanation: Water bodies are homes. Fish swim with fins, frogs live near water, birds catch fish, and plants like lotus and water lily grow in water.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Water body as home

topics.meaning: A food chain shows who eats whom in a habitat.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The chapter uses a river food chain game where students choose roles such as small fish, big fish, frog, bird, human, crocodile or otter and connect themselves using strings. This shows that living things depend on one another for food and balance in freshwater habitats.
topics.easyExplanation: In water, one living thing may become food for another. If one animal disappears, other animals in the chain can be affected.

topics.topic: Origin and Course of a River
topics.meaning: A river begins at a source, flows across land and finally meets another water body.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The chapter follows the Godavari from its origin near Nashik to its delta near the sea, showing how a river travels through forests, towns, fields and plains.
topics.easyExplanation: A river is like a traveller. It begins at a high place, flows through many regions and reaches the sea.
